ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Meat substitutes) is the closest defensible category for gluten-and-soy-based mock fish; no exact fried mock fish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried mock fish or vegetarian fish analogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried mock fish is one piece weighing approximately 80 grams, as commonly served in Singapore Chinese vegetarian restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Mock Fish — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Mock Fish (CR ID: F06020002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Soy protein and wheat gluten composite foods — analogous GI estimate based on soy-based meat analogue data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried mock fish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on the closest analogous foods: soy-based meat analogues and wheat gluten products, which consistently yield GI values in the 28–40 range in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is only 2.5g/100g; glycemic load per 80g serving = (32 × 2.0g) / 100 = 0.6, which is negligible. The low GI is consistent with the dominant protein and fat matrix suppressing glucose response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 617 mg per 100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid fried mock fish due to its sodium content of 617 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict portion to no more than 50g and avoid additional soy-based dipping sauces to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.5g/100g), zero sugar, high protein (21.1g) and dietary fibre (2.6g) support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried mock fish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate and sugar content and high protein and fibre profile; however, the high fat content (24.3g/100g from deep-frying) should be factored into total daily caloric management, and sweet dipping sauces should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (24.3g/100g) from deep-frying and elevated sodium (617 mg/100g) are dual cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume fried mock fish only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high fat from deep-frying and high sodium significantly elevates cardiovascular risk; a steamed or baked preparation with reduced soy sauce would substantially improve the cardiovascular profile of this food.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 617 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fried mock fish is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to high sodium and AGE burden from deep-frying; a steamed preparation with sodium-reduced seasoning would be preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried mock fish is a Chinese vegetarian dish popular in Singapore, made from mashed wheat gluten and soya bean protein shaped to resemble fish, then wrapped in sheets of dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and nori (dried seaweed) before being deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crisp and slightly chewy from the beancurd sheet, while the interior is dense, savoury, and protein-rich from the gluten-soy filling. The seaweed wrapper imparts a subtle umami marine flavour that mimics the appearance and taste of fried fish. It is commonly served in Chinese Buddhist vegetarian restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or soy-based dipping sauces. Nutritionally, fried mock fish is high in protein (21.1g/100g) and fat (24.3g/100g), with very low carbohydrate (2.5g/100g) and zero sugar, making it glycaemically benign. However, its sodium content of 617 mg/100g and high fat from deep-frying make it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It provides meaningful calcium (90 mg/100g) and iron (2.9 mg/100g) from the soy and seaweed components. The food is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, and halal diets but contains gluten and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06020002), which is the primary approved source for Singapore foods. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. Confidence is high.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a savoury processed product with no added sugar and no naturally occurring sugar detected. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fried mock fish preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. GI value of 32 is an analogous estimate based on soy-based meat analogue and wheat gluten product data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for fried mock fish was found. Glycemic load per 80g serving = 0.6 (negligible). The keto dietary tag from the input has been retained in dietary_flags context; however, the high fat content is from deep-frying rather than intentional ketogenic formulation.
